name: Software Developer
verdict: High pay and strong stability. Demand is growing steadily and automation risk is low due to the creative and collaborative nature of the work. One of Canada's strongest career profiles across both wage and security dimensions.

name: Registered Nurse
verdict: Exceptional job security driven by an aging population and persistent nursing shortage. Pay is solid and growing. The physical and emotional demands are real, but near-zero automation risk and permanent employment rates make this one of Canada's most stable career choices.

name: Financial Analyst
verdict: Strong wages but meaningful exposure to automation in routine analysis tasks. AI tools are increasingly handling data aggregation and model-building. The highest-value analysts — those with strategic judgment and client skills — will remain in demand, but mid-tier roles face structural pressure.

name: Pharmacist
verdict: High wages and excellent stability. Dispensing automation exists, but pharmacist scope of practice is expanding — immunizations, medication management, and minor ailment prescribing add value that machines cannot replicate. A strong long-term career with regulated supply constraints supporting wages.

name: Electrician
verdict: The electrification economy is a tailwind. EV infrastructure, solar installations, and new construction are driving persistent demand. Physical, hands-on work cannot be automated. Project-based employment means some income variability, but licensed electricians rarely struggle to find work.

name: Data Scientist
verdict: Top-tier wages and strong near-term growth, but the field is evolving rapidly. Automated ML platforms are absorbing routine modelling tasks, compressing the market for junior data scientists. Senior roles emphasizing business judgment, domain expertise, and communication remain highly valued.

name: Real Estate Agent
verdict: Income is highly variable and commission-dependent. The market correction since 2022 has sharply reduced transaction volumes. Platform disintermediation is ongoing. Top performers earn very well, but median outcomes are modest and income security is among the lowest of any professional occupation.

name: Elementary Teacher
verdict: Excellent stability and meaningful work, offset by wage compression and a lengthy path to permanent positions in many provinces. Summers off and defined-benefit pensions improve the total compensation picture significantly. Work-life quality is above average once tenure is established.

name: Journalist
verdict: The structural decline of Canadian media continues. Newsroom layoffs, advertising revenue collapse, and AI-generated content are compressing both wages and headcount. Freelance journalism is prevalent and financially precarious. Passion is the primary driver for most entrants — the financial case is weak.

name: Civil Engineer
verdict: Infrastructure spending programs across all levels of government are sustaining strong demand. Wages are above the national professional median and PE licensing provides regulatory protection. Design automation tools are augmenting engineers rather than replacing them. A balanced, stable career profile.

name: Social Worker
verdict: Very high job security and growing demand, particularly in mental health, addictions, and child welfare. Wages are the primary limitation — below the university-graduate median despite specialized master's-level credentials. The work is emotionally demanding. Those motivated by impact will find strong employment; those seeking financial returns should look elsewhere.

name: Physician
verdict: The highest-paying career in this dataset. Near-zero unemployment and regulated supply through medical school admissions ensures persistent demand. The decade of training and significant debt load are real barriers. Work-life balance is the primary tradeoff — hours are long and burnout rates are high.

name: Accountant
verdict: Solid wages and good employment stability. CPA designation provides meaningful protection. Routine bookkeeping and compliance work are increasingly automated, but advisory services, audit, and tax strategy require human judgment. The career is evolving, not declining — adaptable accountants remain well-positioned.

name: Welder
verdict: Consistent demand across manufacturing, construction, and energy sectors. Robotic welding handles repetitive production work, but complex, positional, and repair welding continues to require skilled tradespeople. Alberta's energy sector and Ontario manufacturing offer the strongest markets. A reliable mid-tier career.

name: Plumber
verdict: Strong demand driven by construction activity and aging infrastructure. Physical, diagnostic work is essentially impossible to automate at scale. Licensed Red Seal plumbers command premium wages in most markets. Project-based work creates some seasonality but skilled plumbers rarely experience prolonged unemployment.

name: Psychologist
verdict: The mental health crisis has created structural tailwinds for psychologists. Demand for both clinical and organizational psychology is growing. Doctoral-level training is the barrier to entry in most provinces. Those in private practice can achieve strong incomes; institutional roles offer security. Work-life balance is above average.

name: Project Manager
verdict: Good wages and broad applicability across sectors. PM roles are being reshaped by AI-assisted project tools, but coordination, stakeholder management, and risk judgment remain human domains. Certified PMs (PMP) have a significant advantage. Tech-sector PMs earn materially more than average.

name: Graphic Designer
verdict: Generative AI is reshaping the graphic design market rapidly. Routine production work — social assets, templates, basic layouts — is increasingly automated. Designers with strong brand strategy, UX, and motion skills are more insulated, but the entry-level market has structurally contracted. Career longevity requires continuous skill elevation.

name: Marketing Manager
verdict: Decent wages with moderate job security. Marketing is undergoing AI-driven transformation — content generation, ad optimization, and analytics are increasingly automated. Strategic and brand marketing roles are more durable. High variability in outcomes depending on sector: tech marketing pays significantly more than retail or non-profit.

name: Human Resources Manager
verdict: Reasonable stability across most industries. HR technology is automating recruitment screening, payroll, and compliance monitoring, but strategic HR — culture, talent development, executive compensation, labour relations — requires human expertise. CHRP designation is increasingly expected for senior roles.

name: Chef
verdict: One of the most challenging career profiles. Low wages, demanding hours, and high turnover characterize most of the industry. Executive chef roles in fine dining can achieve strong incomes, but these positions are few and the path is long. Labour shortages have marginally improved wages since 2022, but structural challenges remain.

name: Physical Therapist
verdict: Strong job security driven by aging demographics and growing rehabilitation demand. Physical manipulation and patient assessment are inherently hands-on and resistant to automation. Private practice can yield strong earnings above the median. Work-life balance is among the best in healthcare. A well-rounded career profile.

name: Construction Labourer
verdict: Physically demanding work with high injury rates and significant seasonal variability in most provinces. Infrastructure spending provides a degree of demand floor. Unionized labourers on large public projects earn significantly more than non-union counterparts. A stepping stone for many into certified trade apprenticeships.
